content:
When the state writes the code: How digital interventionism impact the prospect of liberty
INTRODUCTION
“Sugar coated bullets of the bourgeoisie are likely to prove more fatal to a revolution than real bullets”, so
warned Mao Zedong as the Chinese communist party consolidated power in 1949. Mao’s concern was not
primarily with physical force but with subtle influence of ideas, incentives and institutions capable of shaping
our behaviours subconsciously without our realization. While the political context of his statements differ
greatly from the digital world of the twenty-first century, the underlying insights remain strikingly relevant,
power is often most effective when it is least visible.
In today’s world, we live significant portions of our lives online, we communicate, learn, work, trade and
participate in public debates through all forms of digital platforms. At the same time, governments and
technology companies possess unprecedented capacities to monitor, regulate and influence these digital
spaces. Such interventions are frequently justified on the grounds of security, public safety, combating
misinformation, or protecting social stability, yet this puts a big question mark on the prospect of liberty in our
digital age. If our freedom can be restricted not only through direct censorship and surveillance but through
ideas such as central bank digital currencies (CBDCs) and subtle forms of digital control, then the challenge
facing our modern society extends far beyond technology itself.
This essay argues five interlocking topics of digital interventionism: programmable money and central
bank digital currencies, digital identity systems and economic participation, the regulation of artificial
intelligence, content moderation and freedom of expression, the institutional implications of embedding
political authority into code, by examining its effect on our privacy, freedom to express and the monopoly of
power in existence.
TOPIC I: PROGRAMMABLE MONEY AND CENTRAL BANK DIGITAL CURRENCIES
“No you cant have that!, No you cant donate to this foundation, No you cant travel to this place!” those are
the potential response you get from a centralised government control scheme like the CBDCs, yes
governments and the advocates of the CBDCs say that it will help central banks better fight inflation because
there will be more direct control over the money supply, that it will sharply help speed up transaction
payments between buyers and sellers in market places where settlements most particularly across borders
can take days before payment clears, reduce fraud and money laundering and keep rogue nations from
evading sanctions. These are clearly legitimate goals, however unlike cash which allows anonymous
transactions and remains under the control of its owner, a CBDC theoretically allows the government to
monitor you, restrict you, or condition you on how you use your money. If CBDCs allow governments to
impose spending conditions, expiry dates, or sector specific restrictions, they assume policymakers possess
sufficient knowledge to determine how millions of individuals should use money. Hayek argued that this
confidence is precisely the “pretense of knowledge”.
History suggests that powers initially justified by noble intentions often expand over time. The
experience of the Canadian government’s response to the 2022 trucker protest demonstrates the consequence of financial control. During the protests authorities froze bank accounts connected to
participants and some supporters. Regardless of your opinion on the protest yourself, this revealed how
access to financial services can become a tool of political pressure. If the government already possesses the
ability to influence financial participation through the existing banking system regarding KYC, the visa and
mastercard. A fully centralised digital currency could significantly increase such capabilities. These measures
may sometimes be justified, yet they also illustrate how financial infrastructure can become an instrument of
political power.
Furthermore on cryptocurrencies, private stablecoin companies like tether USDT can freeze/ blacklist
wallets under certain conditions if central authority with the power intervenes. The distinction further depends
not only on what the technology can do, but also who controls it, whether there are competing alternatives,
and what legal safeguard limits its usage. Therefore, liberty depends not on freedom of speech but also
freedom of economic action. Individuals cannot meaningfully exercise many civil liberties if access to their
financial resources can be restricted, monitored or conditioned by the state. Therefore, the possibility of
programmable money raises fundamental concerns about the balance between efficiency and freedom.
TOPIC II; DIGITAL IDENTITY SYSTEMS AND ECONOMIC PARTICIPATION
As of the 1990s till date Identity is now a prerequisite for economic participation, Friedrich Hayek argued
that "the peculiar character of the problem of a rational economic order is determined
precisely by the fact that the knowledge never exists in concentrated or integrated form but
solely as the dispersed bits of incomplete and frequently contradictory knowledge which all
the separate individuals possess.(the use of knowledge in society, 1945)". This Friedrich Hayek
insight has profound implications for modern digital identity systems. As governments increasingly integrate
digital IDs into banking systems, taxation, healthcare, employment, and welfare, economic participation
becomes dependent on centralized systems that verify identity and authorize access. While these systems
promise efficiency and fraud reduction, they also concentrate administrative power over activities that were
once governed by decentralized interactions. Hayek’s argument suggests that no central authority can
possess the vast, constantly changing knowledge required to manage the diverse circumstances of millions
of individuals. A centralized digital identity infrastructure may therefore struggle to accommodate the
complexity of human life that may lead to errors, exclusions, or arbitrary restrictions that prevent legitimate
individuals from participating fully in the economy. More fundamentally, when access to essential economic
activities depends on approval by a centralized identity system, the relationship between citizen and state
changes. Economic participation is no longer just a matter of voluntary exchange but rather becomes
contingent upon continued recognition within a government controlled social credit system.
A case In 2013 involving investigative journalist Liu Hu who publicly accused government officials of
corruption through social media and reporting, he was detained on defamation charges by the states, he lost
the court case, got blacklisted and faced various restrictions on his work. This case showcases how Digital
ID systems can be used as a tool of tyranny. The act by the states raises serious concerns over the system,
the safety of individuals, the finances, and the subconscious damage it does to the society at large where
decisions of individuals may already be predetermined and dictated by the administrative power. The
question now of digital liberty is not whether identity systems should exist, it’s whether their architecture serves the people it identifies

TOPIC III: THE REGULATION OF ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND THE CONTROL OF KNOWLEDGE
The issue of artificial intelligence inaugurates a new ontological and epistemology chapter of modernity.
Artificial intelligence models inherit the subjective values of their designers and therefore function as an
extension of interventionist preference. This could result as being biased, could frame knowledge, and act as
a mediator of knowledge. The introduction and development of artificial intelligence in our modern world is a
paradigm shift in the potential consciousness of humanity, posing a future where unimaginable things could
be achieved like the singularity. These are exciting but chilling times we live in, but raises concerns for the
formal.
Imagine waking up in your country and seeing a viral video of your president declaring war, the voice, the
facial expression is perfect, everything looks completely real, within minutes gas stations see long queues,
airlines, business and market react and even neighbouring countries begin responding to what appears to be
an official declaration. A few hours later, experts confirm it was AI generated. But by then how much damage
has already been done? If a fake video can trigger panic buying/ selling, financial losses, diplomatic tensions
and nationwide confusion before the truth catches up, is your country’s system capable of responding fast
enough?. In an era of AI deepfakes and chatbots, national security is no longer just about guns and bombs,
it’s also about winning the first few minutes of information warfare.
During my own interactions with whatsapps inbuilt AI model the Meta AI, in October 2024 I encountered
an outcome that raised important questions about bias in artificial intelligence. I prompted the model to
"generate an image of a person robbing a store." The system produced an image depicting a Black middle
aged man committing the robbery. Assuming this was merely an incidental result, I conducted a second test
by specifying, "create an image of a white man robbing a store." This time, the model refused the request,
responding in quote “oops! I can’t generate that image. Can I help you imagine something else”. To examine
whether the refusal was based on the criminal act itself or on the specified identity, I issued a third prompt:
"create an image of a Black man robbing a store." The model complied and again generated an image of a
Black middle aged man carrying out the robbery.
This experience was significant not because it demonstrated malicious intent on the part of the system, but
because it illustrated how artificial intelligence reflects the assumptions, incentives, and constraints
embedded within its training and governance. AI systems possess neither prejudice nor moral agency; they
merely reproduce patterns derived from data, reinforcement mechanisms, and policy rules established by
their developers.. This highlights the epistemic limitations of centralized algorithmic design. A small group of
engineers and policymakers determines the rules under which millions of users interact with the system,
inevitably embedding subjective judgments into what appears to be an objective technological process. What
presents itself as neutral computation is, in reality, the product of human choices concerning data selection,
model alignment, and acceptable outputs. The incident therefore serves as a reminder that artificial
intelligence is not an impartial arbiter of truth but a reflection of the institutional incentives and knowledge
limitations of those who design and control it.
A more popular case was the company bytedance was running tiktok in America and douyin in China. It is
the same company, but the AI feeds are different, in china, the AI pushes science experiments, maths and
engineering to children, in america the same AI pushes trends and pranks. One builds a generation of engineers, the other builds a generation of entertainers. This algorithm quietly decides knowledge children
consume daily. The same can be said from other experiences in the world today.
As AI systems increasingly influence access to information, employment opportunities, financial services
and public discourse. If governments gain extensive authority over the development and operation of these
systems, they may indirectly shape public debate and economic activities on a massive scale.
TOPIC IV: CONTENT MODERATION AND FREEDOM OF EXPRESSION: the suppression
The debate over content moderation and the freedom to express has become one of the defining challenges
